## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, I'm going to take you through an influencer ad takeover campaign. This is the perfect mix of influencer marketing and paid advertising, and the best way to combine your learnings from Nick's module on Facebook ads with the influencer marketing strategies we've been discussing in this module. If you don't do this, you're missing out on a powerful technique that's still really underutilised across the board. So let's dive into it now. I'm going to take you through a case study from the agency Nick was at until recently called Common Threads Collective, which I first saw Nick present on stage at an e-commerce event in Barcelona, which was actually where we first met. So I got him to send across his slides from this event to provide in the resources section for this lesson as well. So even though we've run these campaigns a lot with my brands and our clients at Hay Influencers and I've actually had brands including founder HP and Paypal do this through my account as an influencer as well, what I love about this particular case study is that it's a side-by-side comparison of paying for a post that's running an ad takeover with a very well-known celebrity influencer. So this case study is on a huge scale. It's with an influencer who had 70 million Instagram followers at the time of the collaboration and charged $500,000 US dollars to post to her feed about their brand. So let's start with scenario A, which is the classic influencer marketing scenario of paying for a post. Now let's dig into the data here. So Common Threads assume that her organic reach is about an estimated 15%, which according to them is actually a really aggressive figure. Common reach for macro influencers is close to 3%, but this is a celebrity influencer, so let's assume it's quite a bit higher here. Next up, we know from the research that Common Threads Collective did before engaging her by requesting her audience insights, which I'd highly recommend when working with macro influencers, that only 37% of her audience is based inside the US. This would be fine if you're a global e-commerce brand, but otherwise it's really narrowing down the meaningful reach of the post. Something I'd note again here, which is important, is that just because an influencer is a female based in the US, doesn't always mean that her followers will necessarily be other females based in the US. So remember that an influencer and their audience are not always synonymous, and what we're looking for when finding the right influencers to collaborate with is how similar that influencer's audience is to our brand's target audience. And this is a mistake I often see brands making again and again, thinking that an influencer and their audience are the same. Depending on her content, she may have a much larger than usual male following.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

They could be a lot younger than her, and because she has a celebrity status, as we can see from what we learned through her audience insights, her following is likely to be more global. So this is why we need to request their audience insights, so that we're able to identify whether an influencer's audience is a good fit with what we know about our own target audience. So back to the case study. Next, we're going to look at her engagement rate, which was 4% over the last 10 posts at the time of the case study, which means that you'll reach around 3.9 million people with the post. So to get our CPM, we'll divide 500,000 by 3.9. Then we'll divide it again by 1,000 to get to a CPM of around $128 for US followers. Now let's get to the actual results of the traffic driven to the brand's website from this campaign. The problem with Instagram, which we've already spoken about previously, is that in an organic post, you can't put a link, so you're going to see most of the traffic channeling back through your direct or organic search, which is where you're going to get the most of your uplift. From this, Common Threads' clients are an uplift of 167,000 new visitors to their website, which divided by the $500,000 that they paid for the post in the first place comes to about a $3 cost per click. The thing is that I know a lot of brands like High Smile Teeth Whitening in Australia, for example, who collaborate with influencers like this, like Conor McGregor and Kylie Jenner, and make sure that they have the rights to repurpose the content. And then they'll run this content throughout their ads on their own ad account to build trust, brand affinity, and as a visual hook. So even if you do do a simple product for post, there are definitely still ways to amplify and repurpose this content across your website and online platforms. But moving on, let's talk about scenario B, Advertiser Access, or what I'm referring to in this lesson as an influencer ad takeover. So in this scenario, you get the influencer to create a piece of content, usually a video testimonial, and allow the brand to have Advertiser Access to run dark posts through their page. And what I mean by dark post here is that it means that this post isn't going to show up in the person's news feed, the organic feed, or when you click on the post, you're never going to see the post there. So they're going to maintain that curated look of their profile. As common thread highlights, it's also going to allow them to do a few different things. And I'm pretty much quoting them directly here. First up, the cost to the brand for this content generation plus Advertiser Access was around $250,000. So this was at 50% of the cost of getting them to do a paid post. So within the same budget, you could use the difference to run the paid media for this campaign. We also get to set the targeting, so 100% US or only traffic, for example. We're also going to be able to pull in not only their likes and followers, but you also

## [06:02 - 09:06]

get their page engages, as well as their business account Instagram engages, video viewers off their posts, the list goes on. You can also advertise to your own audiences with that influencer or stack their audience data with your own to create entirely new audiences. And then of course, you get to leverage conversion targeting. This lets Facebook focus on the people that they know will buy from you. You can also scale the spend in the breach as it's not limited to organic reach like it is in the paper post scenario. So this is great content. And as we know, our relevancy score is determined by the quality of the ad and Facebook's incentivized to put great content in front of their clients to keep them on the platform. And the thing is that influencers actually like this method. It's actually a value proposition to them because what you're doing is you're taking them and you're putting them in front of a new audience. This allows them to grow their follower base rather than just exploiting their existing followers. And what I mean by exploiting their existing follower base here is that when you run an influencer campaign where they're posting on your behalf, the influencer needs to take into account and I do this myself, whether this will lose them followers or even worse lose them trust with their existing audience. And the more discerning influencers will either not do this or charge far more for a brand that's not going to be a good fit for their audience. Like I said earlier, founder does this with me and more recently PayPal and HP have two and I'm all for it because it's more awareness around my name and it actually helps me grow my audience as well. So it's really a win-win scenario. So now let's take a look at the difference in response this influencer ad takeover campaign had compared to the first paper post campaign with the exact same influencer. For this influencer takeover campaign, there was a $9.50 CPM as opposed to the previous estimate of $128. The overall reach for this campaign was $26 million, which was nine times as much even with those aggressive estimates. 320,000 visits, which was nearly double the 167,000 from the paid post. So the CPC has more than halved at $1.28. And overall, according to Common Thread, you're going to make around three to four times as many sales for the same amount of money for scenario B compared to scenario A. So as you can see, this union of influencer marketing and Facebook advertising is really powerful and it's definitely something that we want to take advantage of before every brand catches on. As Taylor Holiday, who's the founder of Common Thread Collective says, remember that Facebook and Instagram are human networks. It's people speaking to people. The two greatest influences on what you purchase on the internet are price and a recommendation from people that you trust. And this is exactly where influencers fit in. So as an action item for this lesson, I really want you to execute on this strategy and run

## [09:06 - 09:21]

your first influencer ad takeover. Think back to our lesson on identifying the influential and deciding which influencer to choose and what you've learned with Nick. So go ahead and get your first influencer ad takeover underway and I'll see you in the next lesson.
